subjects.label: Hematoscopy and inspection of drawn blood

works.summary: The passage instructs a physician to inspect blood during and after bloodletting. Thickness, thinness, heat, cold, congealing, harshness, fat, smell, wateriness, foam, surface evenness, color, brittleness, toughness, and bubbles are interpreted as signs of bodily moisture or dryness, corruption, leprosy, liver coldness, wind, burning, mortification, and the predominance of choler, phlegm, melancholy, or other humours.

works.summary: Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition rearranges selected contents of the late-fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century Book of Brome into poetry and puzzles, manorial law, and private accounts. Its literary components include conduct verse, a dice prognostic, Adrian and Epotys, the Brome Abraham and Isaac play, the Fifteen Signs, St Patrick's Purgatory, a fragmentary Life of St Margaret, prayers, a trental direction, and an Annunciation carol. Administrative material includes common and tax lists, church duties, twelve paired-language legal model documents, court articles, accounts, a 1504 agreement, and an incomplete jaundice recipe.

works.summary: Julian of Norwich's extended prose account of sixteen revelations received during severe illness in May 1373 and contemplated over subsequent decades. works.regionDisplay: England, with London specifically associated with the civic chronicle; exact place of compilation unknown
works.summary: A manuscript-specific historical sequence surviving in copies made circa 1465. It opens with an abbreviated prose Brut running from the legendary daughters of Diocletian and Brutus through the first year of Henry IV. It then copies John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England through Henry VI as prose, followed without a new XML division by a London civic chronicle organized under keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs from Richard I through Edward IV's fourth year. The final entries report the capture of Henry VI and Edward IV's new coinage. James Gairdner first printed the sequence in 1880.

publication.embeddedTitle: Jyl of Breyntford's Testament … And Other Short Pieces.

works.regionDisplay: West Midlands (scribal dialect and witness context; composition place unknown)
works.summary: An eavesdropping narrator reports a gathering of ten wives at an alehouse with no men present. Over wine, the wives take turns describing their husbands’ genitalia and sexual inadequacies through curses, comparisons, and explicit comic complaint. The poem combines fabliau and social satire with a scene of female fellowship, shared sexual knowledge, and consolation, even as its male-framed representation draws on misogynist stereotypes of women’s drunken and unruly speech.

publication.embeddedTitle: Treatise on The Astrolabe: addressed to his son Lowys / by Geoffrey Chaucer; edited from the earliest MSS. by Walter W. Skeat

works.regionDisplay: England; exact place of composition not securely established
works.summary: A Middle English prose manual explaining the parts and operation of a planispheric astrolabe for the learner Lowys. The core describes the instrument and gives numbered procedures for finding solar and stellar positions, time, latitude, ascensions, planetary positions, and related astronomical quantities. Skeat's edition appends eleven conclusions from other manuscripts whose Chaucerian authorship is doubtful.

works.regionDisplay: York, Selby, and New Malton in Yorkshire and northern England

subjects.label: Building survey, property boundaries, and urban drainage

works.summary: This composite selection brings together five distinct groups of late medieval northern English records. A 1428 York proceeding investigates adulterated iron, tin, and other trade goods; twenty-one York searchers' verdicts regulate encroachments, boundaries, gutters, walls, and building obligations from 1417 to 1501; eight selected Selby manorial jury presentments report local offences and obligations in the 1470s and 1480s; twenty-one York-enrolled certificates address the English parentage and birth of people accused of being Scots from 1476 to 1497; and a fifteenth-century text sets out the customs and liberties of New Malton burgesses. Raine's modern editorial selection is valuable for civic government, craft regulation, urban property, manorial jurisdiction, identity, documentary practice, and borough custom, but it does not make these records one medieval work.

After a society source note and Mackerell's introduction, it prints guild ordinances governing worship, livery, feasting, office, discipline, poor relief, and burial; an English rendering of Henry V's 1417 incorporation and mortmain patent; and the 1452 agreement that joined the guild's leadership and membership to Norwich civic office.

subjects.label: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment

works.summary: Furnivall's 1878 EETS selection brings together five distinct Middle English verse works: Adam Davy's politically prophetic Five Dreams; four versions of the Life of St Alexius printed from six manuscripts; selected Old Testament History passages centered on Solomon and later biblical history; the Fifteen Signs and its continuous Lamentacio; and a separately copied prologue to the Conception of Mary. The selection is cataloged as an editorial collection while preserving each work, version branch, witness stream, and non-contiguous excerpt as a scoped part.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose guide addresses an unnamed traveler of means and his entourage. It advises secrecy, economy, reliable couriers and hosts, routes from Calais and Bruges toward Venice, favorable seasons and shipping to Alexandria or the Levant, relations with Venetian and local authorities, interpreters, food, water, bedding, medicines, heat, disease, piracy, and possible onward or return routes through Rhodes, Constantinople, the Black Sea, Sicily, Iberia, and Portugal. The surviving text begins in mid-instruction and contains no named author or recipient.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English defence of positions associated with early Lollardy. After a prologue in which the first-person speaker submits every claim to Scripture and correction, the treatise responds to objections concerning papal and clerical authority, indulgences, excommunication, preaching and priestly ministry, the Mass and sacraments, poverty, fasting and prayer, churches and images, simony and Antichrist, marriage and canon law, discipleship, vows, charms, religious labor, and begging. Its method repeatedly tests ecclesiastical practice against Scripture, patristic authorities, and canon law.

works.summary: Herrtage's 1879 compilation presents several late-medieval English versions of the Gesta Romanorum in a deliberately comparative arrangement. Its short prose narratives draw on classical and biblical names, legends, miracles, wonders, romance and folktale motifs, domestic and political dilemmas, and exemplary reversals. Most stories proceed to a Christian moralization that interprets emperors, knights, animals, objects, journeys, crimes, and rewards as figures for God, Christ, the Church, confession, penance, temptation, virtue, vice, judgment, death, and salvation. The edition's importance lies as much in its juxtaposition of divergent Harley, Additional, and Kk.1.6 streams and de Worde selections as in any single normalized tale sequence.

works.summary: Emaré, daughter of Emperor Artyus, refuses her father's attempt to marry her and is set adrift wearing a marvelous gemmed and embroidered robe. Her husband and later her repentant father come to Rome on pilgrimage or penance; Segramour helps reveal her identity, and the separated family is reconciled.

works.summary: The English Conquest of Ireland is an anonymous Hiberno-Middle English prose retelling of Gerald of Wales's Expugnatio Hibernica. It narrates the Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest from 1166 to 1185 for a late-medieval English readership in Ireland. The translator worked from an unidentified intermediate recension rather than a securely recovered Latin exemplar and reshaped the source through condensation, omission, reordering, and expansion while preserving its broad historical sequence and colonial perspective.

works.regionDisplay: England, especially London, Norfolk, Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, and Tettenhall, with records from many additional towns

subjects.label: History and origins of trade unions

works.summary: English Gilds assembles selected returns, ordinances, customaries, charters, reports, and municipal records to document the organization and social practices of English religious, craft, and civic guilds. Part I prints forty-nine English returns from the 1388–89 royal inquiry, chiefly from London and Norfolk. Part II broadens the evidence through translated and English materials from towns across England. Part III presents four separate civic texts: Winchester's Olde Usages, Worcester ordinances, Robert Ricart's account of the Bristol mayor's office, and the Tettenhall customary. A fuller Norwich St George ordinance set and Richard Morris's grammatical notes form the two appendices. Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction frames the documentary project, while Lujo Brentano's independent five-part essay argues a history from early guilds to trade unions. The complete 1870 edition also contains Lucy's glossarial index and a general index, both absent from the repository XML.

works.regionDisplay: England; translated and adapted from the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc tradition of the Low Countries

subjects.label: The transience and desertion of worldly attachments

works.summary: God sends Death to summon Everyman to a final reckoning. Fellowship, Kindred, Cousin, and Goods refuse his journey; Good Deeds is initially too weak to accompany him, but Knowledge leads him through Confession and penance until Good Deeds can rise. Beauty, Strength, Discretion, and Five Wits pledge aid but abandon him at the grave. Only Good Deeds enters with him, while Knowledge remains to hear the angels welcome his soul. A Doctor's epilogue directs hearers to reject pride and prepare their accounts before death. The English work translates and reshapes the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc into an allegorical meditation on mortality, sacramental preparation, charity, and salvation.

works.summary: Kingsford selected three politically and autobiographically distinctive passages from Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. The dedication and proem present the book to Henry VI while rehearsing Hardyng's Scottish evidence and unrewarded service; the praise of Henry V turns royal commemoration into counsel about peace and law; and the conclusion praises Robert Umfraville, urges the king to defend common profit, renews Hardyng's grievance, and closes with two envoy stanzas. The selection is a direct transcription from the sole first-version witness, not a multi-witness critical edition.

works.summary: Generides, the unacknowledged son of King Aufreus of India and Sereyne of Syria, enters his father's service, resists the adulterous queen Sirenydes, and leaves for Persia after the traitor Amalek turns her accusation against him. He wins distinction in war and the love of the sultan's daughter Clarionas, defeats rivals and invading kings, helps restore Aufreus, survives further betrayals and separations, and ultimately marries Clarionas and succeeds to kingdoms in India and Persia. The romance combines dynastic restoration, courtly love, battlefield prowess, disguise and recognition, loyal friendship, and providential prediction.

works.summary: After Saracen invaders kill King Murry and seize Suddene, his son Horn and twelve companions are set adrift and reach Westernesse. Horn grows into a knight and pledges himself to Rymenhild, but the traitor Fikenhild falsely accuses him and causes a second exile. In Ireland Horn defeats a Saracen champion and gathers allies; he returns in disguise to prevent Rymenhild's forced marriage, reconquers Suddene, restores Christian rule and his inheritance, and finally rescues Rymenhild from Fikenhild. works.summary: La Estorie del Evangelie is an anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ. It combines Gospel narrative from the Annunciation and Nativity through the Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, and Last Judgment with devotional exhortation, bestiary moralization, apocryphal episodes, and recurring Latin rubrics. CME00079 serializes Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 parallel edition of two substantially different manuscript versions: an early 519-line Dulwich fragment and a longer, heavily revised Bodleian text. Campbell's modern English introduction and concluding source notes are omitted. Modern scholarship recognizes seven mutually non-derivative surviving witnesses; the other five are recorded as collateral transmission evidence rather than sources of the two encoded texts.

works.summary: The legend recounts Mary Magdalene's noble family, worldly life, repentance, intimacy with Christ, and place at the Resurrection. Their pilgrimage to Saint Peter includes the wife's death in childbirth, miraculous preservation of mother and child, and restoration on the return voyage. Mary then converts communities, withdraws for thirty years into a desert retreat where angels raise and feed her, receives the Eucharist, and dies. The Durham copy breaks as Maximinus begins to anoint her body.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English catechetical and devotional prose dialogue on how the soul reaches heaven. Biblical quotation and allusion carry much of the argument, while repeated emphasis on motive presents good works as the fruit of love rather than an external checklist.

works.summary: The Lucidarie is an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a disciple questions a master about Christian doctrine. It selectively adapts much of Book I and a small portion of Book II of the Latin Elucidarium, omits Book III, and inserts fourteen additional exchanges. Subjects range from the Trinity, creation, angels, the Fall, incarnation, redemption, Eucharist, and priesthood to clerical corruption, religious poverty, Antichrist, sin, free will, grace, Mary, and the saints. Although earlier scholarship identified the work as Lollard, Anna Lewis argues that its mixture of orthodox and potentially heterodox positions makes that label misleading; possible influence is not converted into a categorical work classification.

Walls, stones, towers, steeple, weathercock, cross, aisles, windows, pillars, seats, steps, and altar signify the church militant and triumphant, Scripture, clergy, laity, contemplation, charity, and Christ. The poem opens with the first three commandments, urges reverence and disciplined conduct in church, and closes by excluding pride, lechery, and covetousness from the living edifice of Christian people.

works.summary: Speculum Vitae is an anonymous Middle English catechetical poem of 16,096 four-stress lines in rhyming couplets. It organizes Christian teaching through the words and seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer, treating the Trinity, Decalogue, Creed, sacraments, virtues and vices, seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, beatitudes, and related moral instruction. Composed in Yorkshire in the third quarter of the fourteenth century, it selectively translates and rearranges Laurent d'Orléans's Somme le roi and incorporates material from the Latin Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice. The represented Additional MS 33995 copy names no author; the former attribution to William of Nassyngton rests on an interpolation in other manuscripts and is rejected by modern scholarship.

works.summary: After seven childless years, the emperor Octavian's wife bears twin sons. The emperor's mother frames the empress for adultery, and mother and children are exiled rather than burned. In the wilderness the twins are carried away by animals. The empress reaches Jerusalem with one child, the younger Octavian, while the other is sold to the Parisian burgess Clement and raised as Florent. Florent's noble instincts conflict comically with Clement's mercantile values; he defeats a Saracen giant, is recognized by his father, and loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle. When the Christian rulers are captured, the younger Octavian travels from Jerusalem with his mother and her lioness, defeats the Saracens, and reunites the family. Marsabelle converts and marries Florent, and the emperor's treacherous mother kills herself.

works.summary: Henryson recounts Orpheus's lineage, marriage to Eurydice, and grief after she is taken to the underworld. Orpheus searches the planetary spheres, learns celestial harmony, descends through hell, charms its guardians and tormented figures, and wins Eurydice's release on condition that he not look back; his backward glance loses her again. A long Moralitas then interprets Orpheus as the intellectual or reasoning power of the soul and Eurydice as affection or appetite, turning the classical romance into instruction about sin, eloquence, worldly desire, contemplation, and the discipline of reason.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English alliterative satire in which a narrator seeking to learn the Apostles' Creed consults representatives of the four mendicant orders. Their rivalry, wealth, and demands for money expose their failure as spiritual teachers. The poor plowman Piers instead explains true Christian belief and recites the Creed, joining antifraternal polemic to a Wycliffite ideal of apostolic poverty and plain faith.

works.regionDisplay: King's Lynn and Norfolk, England; the compiler explicitly followed Norfolk usage, while the Harley base witness is probably not East Anglian
works.summary: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction and designed for learners who needed ready access to Latin vocabulary, the Promptorium parvulorum pairs Middle English headwords and explanatory phrases with Latin equivalents and grammatical information. Its compiler identifies himself only as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn who followed Norfolk speech. The conventional name Geoffrey the Grammarian is a later, disputed attribution; Richard Fraunces is a plausible but unverified alternative. The medieval arrangement separates nomina from verba within alphabetic sections; Way's immediate source edition instead merges them into one alphabetic sequence and adds extensive commentary.

works.summary: A hagiographic selection centered on saints, miracles, and devotional practice. Caxton's Winifred narrative recounts the Welsh virgin's attempted sexual coercion, decapitation, miraculous restoration, religious life, death, and the translation of her relics to Shrewsbury; Mirk retells the life and shrine miracles as a festal sermon. Fifteen Marian exempla emphasize prayer and intercession. Dorothy's life narrates virgin martyrdom and the winter roses and apples sent to Theophilus. The Jerome cycle combines a life, pseudo-epistolary visions, descriptions of the afterlife, and posthumous miracles.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts Richard I's life and the Third Crusade as heroic, marvelous, and violently polemical narrative. In the expansive a version represented by Brunner's main reading, Richard's demon-mother legend, feats of strength, crusading battles, sieges, and notorious cannibalism episode build an idealized English warrior king while dehumanizing Muslim opponents. The materially different b tradition omits or reshapes important episodes, and the surviving witnesses resist reduction to a single linear form.

subjects.label: Conjuration, exorcism, and discernment of the dead

works.summary: During rebuilding at St Paul's Cathedral, workers discover an undeciphered marble tomb containing the incorrupt body of a richly dressed man. Bishop Erkenwald summons the corpse to speak. It identifies itself as a just pagan judge of ancient New Troy who was honored for impartial judgment but remained excluded from salvation because he had never received Christian baptism. As Erkenwald utters the Trinitarian baptismal formula, his tears fall on the corpse and become baptismal water; the released soul enters heaven, the body decays, and London responds with procession and bells.

works.summary: The volume joins two large sections of the English Wycliffite sermon cycle with two polemical biblical expositions. The ferial Gospel sermons and Sunday Epistle sermons explain the readings of the church year in Middle English while repeatedly applying scripture to belief, pastoral practice, clerical conduct, property, religious orders, and reform. Vae Octuplex applies Matthew 23's eight woes to friars and prelates. works.summary: At Arthur's New Year feast, a supernatural green knight proposes a beheading exchange. Gawain accepts, strikes the first blow, and a year later journeys to keep the return appointment. The Green Knight reveals the tests at the Green Chapel, and Gawain returns to Camelot chastened by the gap between chivalric ideals and human conduct.

works.summary: Torrent, an earl's son in Portugal, loves Princess Desonell, but her father repeatedly tries to prevent the match by sending him on lethal quests. Torrent defeats giants, a dragon, and rival champions across an expansive romance geography. After Desonell bears twins, her father casts mother and children to sea; the boys are separately rescued and raised in Jerusalem and Greece, while Desonell reaches Nazareth. Torrent returns, joins crusading warfare, encounters his sons without recognizing them, and ultimately reunites with his family through tournaments, stories, rings, and other tokens of recognition. The lovers marry, their sons inherit kingdoms, and Torrent is elected emperor.

works.summary: A late Middle English theological dialogue in which Pecock seeks a rational way to restore lay dissenters to obedience without grounding that obedience in ecclesiastical infallibility. The prologue frames the work as a response to English lay readers who privilege their own reading of Scripture over clerical teaching. Part I defines faith, argues that evidence and reason strengthen rather than destroy it, and maintains that Christians owe obedience to the church unless its error can be publicly and irrefutably proved. Part II tests the relation between church and Scripture through arguments about oral tradition, apostolic teaching, interpretation, the Apostles' Creed, and whether the clergy can create new articles of faith. The sole manuscript loses two leaves internally and breaks off during Part II chapter 5.

works.summary: The Desert of Religion is an anonymous Northern Middle English devotional poem that turns religious life into a spiritual wilderness and forest. Its rhyming couplets organize catechetical and ascetic teaching—virtues and vices, humility and pride, commandments, sacraments, works of mercy, confession, contemplation, and the perils of temptation—around twenty allegorical trees. In all three surviving fifteenth-century manuscripts, tree diagrams and images of saints and hermits form an integral visual program that invites meditative reading. The author-compiler openly says the treatise was taken from many books; Hope Emily Allen and Anne Mouron identify the Speculum vitae as its chief source alongside The Prick of Conscience, Richard Rolle's Emendatio vitae, and other devotional materials. CME00075 represents Hübner's 1911 edited poem and apparatus together with his German manuscript description and afterword, K. Schreiner's German textual and linguistic criticism, and Hübner's German source study; those editorial prose sections are retained context, not medieval authorship.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts the Roman destruction of Jerusalem as Christian divine vengeance for Christ's death. The surviving Pepys short-version text begins after its lost or omitted opening, recounts portents and the martyrdom of James, the illnesses and conversions of Vespasian and Titus, Veronica's image-bearing cloth, the Roman siege, famine and cannibalism, Josephus's escape, and Titus's healing, then breaks off before the wider romance's conclusion. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, torture, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed. The encoded unit concludes with two German prose paragraphs in which Fischer comments on the partial version and its relation to the wider Vengeaunce tradition.

works.summary: Parallel betrayals displace the child heirs of England and Denmark. Earl Goodrich imprisons the English princess Goldborou, while Earl Godard murders Havelok's sisters and orders the fisherman Grim to drown him. Grim recognizes Havelok's royal birthmark and miraculous light, escapes with him to Lincolnshire, and raises him at Grimsby. Havelok later labors in Lincoln, is forced into marriage with Goldborou, and learns through signs and dreams that both are royal heirs. Disguised as merchants, they return to Denmark, win Earl Ubbe's loyalty, defeat Godard, and restore Havelok's Danish kingship. Havelok then returns to England, defeats Goodrich, restores Goldborou, and rules with her after their coronation.

Driven by wounded status and despair, he seeks a Jewish magician, renounces Christ and the Virgin, and signs a pact with the devil. Repenting, he undertakes penance and prays to Mary, who intercedes for him and restores the written pact. After public confession and communion, he dies reconciled. The Northern Homily Cycle uses the narrative as an exemplum of pride, sin, penance, confession, Marian intercession, mercy, and salvation.

works.summary: Theophilus, an archdeacon proud of his wealth and rank, suddenly loses his prosperity. An unnamed Jewish intermediary offers to restore him and leads him to Satan, whom Theophilus serves after renouncing Christ and Mary and signing a charter in his own blood. His rank and wealth restored, Theophilus later hears the heavenly command 'Revertere', repents, and appeals to the Virgin. Mary descends to hell, overcomes Satan, retrieves the charter, and returns it to Theophilus, who closes with praise of her miracles. The poem reshapes a Marian miracle as a compact tail-rhyme narrative and contains a harmful anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype. CME00082 retains Heuser's German introduction and editorial comments around the complete Middle English poem, but those modern frames are not part of the cataloged medieval work.

works.summary: Ipomydon, heir to Apulia, hears of the beauty and prowess of the lady who rules Calabria and enters her service without revealing his royal identity. He excels in hunting and wins a great tournament while appearing on successive days in white, red, and black, yet conceals his victories and departs before accepting marriage. When the lady is later threatened by Duke Geron, Ipomydon returns disguised as a fool, secures the right to answer her appeal for aid, defends her messenger from three attackers, defeats Geron, and is temporarily mistaken for him. A combat leads to recognition of his long-lost half-brother Campanus; Ipomydon's disguises and earlier deeds are revealed, he reunites with the lady, and the romance closes with marriage, kingship, rewards, and a prayer for salvation.

works.summary: Pecock answers lay and Lollard criticism of eleven clerical practices by insisting that blame must be grounded in patient, demonstrable doctrine. The first part develops his general case for reasoned moral judgment and against treating private scriptural interpretation as self-validating. The remaining parts defend images and pilgrimage; relics and liturgical practices; clerical landholding and endowment; papal and episcopal hierarchy and church law; and religious orders, while referring several remaining controversies to his other writings. The work does not excuse genuine clerical wrongdoing: its polemical target is criticism Pecock regards as unproved or misdirected.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French verse romance. The fairy Melusine marries Raymond of Poitiers on condition that he never seeks to see her on Saturdays, builds his power and dynasty, and bears sons whose adventures extend the Lusignan lineage across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymond's breach of secrecy exposes Melusine's serpentine transformation and separates the couple; later quests, combats, family catastrophes, dynastic praise, prayer, and the translator's reflections on poetic translation bring the poem to its close.

works.summary: A two-poem editorial selection from Laud Misc. 108. The Sayings of St. Bernard urges self-knowledge and penance through reminders of bodily decay, conflict between flesh and soul, the three enemies of humankind, worldly vanity, unjust possessions, and death, ending with an appeal to Mary. The Vision of St. Paul follows Paul and Michael through punishments in hell, connects penalties to particular sins, recounts the removal of a soul to damnation and the reception of a good soul, and culminates in Christ granting the damned respite from Saturday noon until Monday morning before a closing prayer for grace at judgment. Horstmann prints both anonymous six-line stanzaic works directly from adjacent Laud leaves with German headings and textual notes.

A clerical translator addresses a spiritual daughter, explains that he omits material unsuited to her and other devout readers, rearranges and renders the sense rather than the words of the Latin, and organizes the result as seven points corresponding to the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit. subjects.label: Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English alliterative romance recasts the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem as Christian vengeance for Christ's death. It joins the healing and conversion of Titus and Vespasian to a long military narrative of factional conflict, famine, cannibalism, mass death, enslavement, and the destruction of the Temple. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed.

works.summary: On the fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury pilgrimage, Lydgate joins the company and is required by the Host to tell the first return tale. He recounts the foundation and destruction of Thebes: the history of Oedipus, the divided rule and fatal quarrel of Eteocles and Polynices, the embassy and exploits of Tydeus, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and the city's ruin. The poem repeatedly turns legendary history into counsel about prudent rule, truthful promises, peace, civic unity, and the destructiveness of private ambition.

works.summary: The first poem is a twenty-stanza alliterative hymn to Saint Katherine of Alexandria. It recounts her disputation with fifty rhetoricians, their conversion and martyrdom, the conversions and deaths of the empress and Porphyry, Katherine's torture and beheading, the translation of her body to Sinai, and her power to intercede, before closing in direct prayer. Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. The second and formally independent poem praises five joys of Mary—the Annunciation, Christ's Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Mary's Assumption, and Coronation—and ends by asking her intercession. Its stanza initials spell MARIA and the six-line envoy spells PIPWEL.

works.summary: Kingsford's edited collection reconstructs a dispersed archive associated with the Stonor family, established Oxfordshire gentry whose legal, estate, household, mercantile, familial, and political networks extended across England and beyond. The selected records range from a thirteenth-century charter to Francis Lovell's letter of 11 October 1483 and include family and business correspondence, deeds, petitions, writs, accounts, rentals, inventories, and other records. The collection is especially rich for fifteenth-century gentry life, literacy and letter writing, estate and household management, legal practice, commerce and the wool trade, and political experience during the Wars of the Roses.

subjects.label: Idolatry and renunciation of pagan gods

works.summary: A lady asks an aging poet to translate Aseneth's story from Latin into English. The narrative tells how Aseneth, proud daughter of the Egyptian priest Potiphar, rejects men and worships idols until she sees Joseph. Joseph refuses to kiss an idolater, prompting her seven-day fast, repentance, and prayer. A heavenly visitor accepts her conversion and performs the miracle of a honeycomb and bees; Joseph and Aseneth marry. Later Pharaoh's son conspires with Dan and Gad to abduct her, but Benjamin, Simeon, and Levi defeat the attack. Aseneth intercedes for the conspirators, and Joseph eventually governs Egypt before returning the crown. The poem closes by naming itself a rude translation and asking the patron's pleasure. A 49-line lament for a young gentlewoman follows in the unique manuscript; MacCracken printed it as an epilogue, but its original attachment remains disputed.

works.regionDisplay: Scotland; narrative set during the Trojan War
works.summary: After invoking Chaucer and another account of Cresseid, the narrator tells how she is abandoned by Diomede, denounces the gods, and is judged in her dream by a planetary parliament with leprosy and exclusion. At the leper hospital she laments her fall in a seven-stanza complaint; afterward, while begging, she encounters Troilus without either recognizing the other. Once told his identity, she makes a testament to the lepers and dies; Troilus receives her ring and erects her tomb. Henryson transforms the unresolved fate of Chaucer's Criseyde into a Scots tragedy about mutability, social judgment, repentance, compassion, and literary authority.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose translation and abridgment of John of Hildesheim's Historia trium regum. It begins with prophecies and traditions surrounding the Magi, narrates their journey to Bethlehem and Adoration of Christ, follows their later conversion, preaching, deaths, and association with Saint Thomas, and traces the legendary transfer of their relics through Constantinople and Milan to Cologne. works.summary: An anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Vision of Tundale. Tundale, a wealthy and violent Irish knight, collapses at a meal and appears dead; separated from his body, his soul is guided by an angel through escalating punishments for sin, Hell and Lucifer, milder purgatorial states, earthly-paradisal joys, and a glimpse of Heaven. Returned to life, he confesses, receives the sacrament, gives away his goods, preaches what he has seen, and lives penitently. The poem makes divine justice, mercy, punishment, and conversion central to its account of the Christian afterlife.

works.summary: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within an account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, royal patrons, and institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, shrine and relic miracles, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its female religious community. Its joined hagiography and history constructs a usable past for the abbey rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

subjects.label: Wilton Abbey, its founders, and convent identity

works.summary: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within a long account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, its royal patrons, and the abbey's institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, miracles associated with her shrine and relics, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its community. Its combination of hagiography and history constructs a usable past for a female religious house rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

works.summary: An anonymous English adaptation of Æthelwold's Old English Rule of St Benedict for a female religious community. A prologue frames obedience and conversion as the way to eternal life; seventy-three chapters regulate kinds of religious, the abbess, counsel, humility, worship, discipline, property, food and drink, work and reading, hospitality, admission, offices, travel, mutual obedience, and communal charity. The work remains textually and syntactically close to its Old English base while updating forms and rewriting some passages.

works.summary: A wright marries a reputedly faithful woman and receives a rose garland whose color is said to reveal her fidelity. While he is away building for his lord, the lord, steward, and proctor each offer his wife money for sex. She accepts their gifts, traps each would-be lover in a deep chamber, and makes the three men process flax and spin in exchange for food. When the wright returns, the men are exposed and released, the wife keeps their money, and the unfaded garland confirms her fidelity.

works.regionDisplay: England; exact place of composition unknown
works.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose treatise presenting angling as a healthful and morally regulated recreation. After comparing fishing favorably with hunting, hawking, and fowling, it teaches the making and coloring of rods and lines, hooks, floats, methods, suitable places, times and weather, impediments, fish-specific and seasonal baits, and the care of bait. Its final order prohibits trespass, theft, and excessive taking, urges stewardship of fish stocks, and frames solitary angling as conducive to bodily health, prayer, and avoidance of idleness. Two brief Latin maxims—four lines total—are quoted and explained in Middle English. The CME text represents Satchell's 1883 composite edition, which joins two surviving manuscript runs to two passages supplied from the 1496 printed Treatyse.

works.summary: Pope Gregory's mother, revered for outward holiness, confesses that shame kept her from revealing secret sexual sin and the killing of three children. After death she returns as a tormented, foul-smelling ghost and asks Gregory to perform a prescribed trental of thirty masses, the Office of the Dead, and prayer. When the observance is completed she returns radiant among angels and announces her release into bliss. CME00102 represents Bülbring's 190-line Edinburgh-witness version and his commentary; a following two-line statement about fidelity to the copy is a separately indexed scribal saying, not part of the poem.

works.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose version of the Visio Pauli. Saint Paul and the archangel Michael pass through Hell, where they see souls punished for sexual sins, usury, pride and covetousness, religious negligence, unbelief, harm to others, and failure to repent. The damned ask Paul and Michael to intercede. Christ rebukes their wickedness in life but, through the prayer of Michael and Paul and for his Resurrection, grants them a recurring respite from Saturday at noon until sunrise on Monday. The work closes with Paul and Michael suddenly departing the place of sorrow.
